title: Week 6 : CRITIQUE OF THE LITERATURE WITH JULIE BURING
text:
hi everyone and thanks for coming back
this week we've been talking about
experimental studies and I've been
giving some lectures about various
aspects of experimental studies now what
we'd like to do is to give you an
opportunity to critique a published
paper using a uh that use an
experimental design and what I've asked
is my good friend Julie burring a fellow
epidemiologist uh to lead this
discussion I've known Julie for many
years we were students together she is
currently teaching an introductory
epidemiology course here at the school
which is very similar to what I'm doing
here so but what today I've asked Julie
to do is to talk about a particular
paper that she's going to ask you to
read also you've seen Julie already
she's been giving some of the lectures
on a large experimental study that she
and her colleagues are doing here at
Harvard but today it's the the um aim is
to take you through the a paper that
we're going to ask you to read and then
after that we're going to ask you to go
to the uh student discussion forum and
answer some questions that she's going
to pose to you so as a group you can
discuss uh this paper and then later on
she's going to come back and give you
her answers to those questions as well
as incorporating some of the comments
that you've had so Julie before we start
can you tell us when did you ever decide
to become an epidemiologist what drove
you to enter this field uh if you can
give us some background about that and
then take us through the paper you want
us to discuss so Julie Flor is yours
thank you Fran actually I had never even
heard the word epidemiology until I went
to graduate school so in college at
Pomona College in California I was a
math major but I was always interested
in math applying it to something so I
started to do statistics and when I
graduated I wanted to go on and get a
degree graduate degree in biostatistics
so I went to the University of
Washington in Seattle I got my Master's
there but I realized again that I wanted
to be able to apply those tools to the
design conduct and interpretation of
studies so in fact I changed into the
field of epidemiology which to me was
very much like Medical detectives trying
to sort out how we would ever know why
one factor or how one factor affected
the risk of developing an outcome I came
back to the Harvard School of Public
Health I got my doctorate in
epidemiology here and I've been in this
community ever since I'm a professor of
medicine at and Women's Hospital in
Harvard Medical School and a professor
of epidemiology at the Harvard School of
Public Health in terms of my research
Focus what I'm really most interested in
is the concept of prevention what can
someone do who doesn't have a disease
yet a chronic disease like
cardiovascular disease and cancer what
can they do to reduce their risk of
getting those diseases for the very
first time and whether it be taking a
vitamin supplement and wondering whether
that will make a difference or not
changing their lifestyle avoiding an
environmental exposure what can we do to
reduce our risk of developing chronic
disease for the very first time and to
accomplish that I have been working
primarily with randomized clinical trial
methodology where basically you take a
large group of people and you divide
them into two subgroups one who actually
gets the active intervention like a
vitamin or eating a particular way and
the other group does not get that
intervention and you compare the two
groups over time and see whether they
differ in terms of the number of
outcomes that they got that have are of
interest to you so that's the
methodology I've been using and for
today when we're actually going to be
critiquing an article I chose a
randomized clinical trial that was of a
very practical question um that many of
us have asked ourselves over time in
particular if I have to put on a
Band-Aid and I want to remove that
Band-Aid will it hurt me less if I
remove it rapidly or if I remove it
slowly and that's sort of the question
that the trial that we're going to talk
about today is trying to
answer so the particular article that
you will be reading is called Fast
versus slow Band-Aid removal a
randomized trial It's actually an
article that comes out of Australia and
I picked it for two reasons I picked it
because when we were reading it we would
cly identify with the issue that they
are the question that they are asking
and I think we'll also recognize that
randomized trials can be used to answer
a whole host of questions so it can be a
drug it can be a lifestyle it can be a
way that we live such as this article so
to really recognize the contribution of
randomized clinical trials and clinical
research the other thing that I want to
do through the questions that you're
going to be looking at and thinking
about is to recognize the key features
of the design the conduct and the
interpretation and Analysis of
randomized
Trials so just for a second let me set
the stage for this trial that you're
going to be reading the fast versus slow
Band-Aid removal it is a randomized
trial comparing two groups fast Band-Aid
removal which is called FB R and slow
Band-Aid removal which is
sbar they used a study population which
is actually just a convenient sample the
study was done on in Australia they went
to James Cook University Medical School
there they took 65 volunteers from the
second and the third years of medical
school and that was their
population then they applied an
intervention they put they put on the
participants mediumsized
Band-Aids this size medium-size
Band-Aids it's the kind of Band-Aid that
we use very very often and they applied
them bilaterally so they applied them on
the left side as well as the right side
in three body locations where we often
have Band-Aids on the arm on the hand
and on the
ankle and the intervention was to have
this Band-Aid on and then to remove it
in what would they called a crossover
design so every person had two Band-Aids
on their arm two Banda bands on their
hand two Band-Aids on their ankle they
removed both of the Band-Aids on the arm
one of them was removed fast one of them
was removed slowly and they randomized
them to which arm would have the fast or
the slow removal and so it's called a
crossover because it's the same person
that had both of the interventions the
fast versus the slow and it's randomized
because you didn't always just do the
right arm first and the left arm second
you were randomized to which one would
be removed and then they went ahead and
followed the individuals to see which
which arm which hand which ankle the
participants felt had the less pain
least pain less pain the one that was
fast removal or the one that was slow
removal so again if I could ask Fran to
come up for just a second I will just
show you how they actually did the study
so on the arm they put on a
Band-Aid in a crossover design which
means that Fran's other arm was the
other group in the study we put on
another Band-Aid in the exact same
location we put it on nice and tight all
the little hairs that are underneath it
that ought to drive FR crazy when I pull
this
off I would be randomized to one arm
going being removed slowly and one fast
but for now I'm just going to pick one
and do it the fast removal is that you
pick up the Band-Aid and just rip it off
as fast as you can the slow removal is
that you do it steadily but over a 2
second period so for the slow removal
hey guys before she does this uh you'll
notice I am one of those hairy
individuals I have hair over my entire
body except the top of my head the
bottom of my feet and my palms and she
has has really chosen hair pots in my
body and Julie is a good friend so
that's why I'm willing to do this this
is active learning at is extremes okay
Julie so should I close my eyes so I
don't know what you're doing you can do
whatever want you want so for the fast
one first I have to get an edge to it
clearly I have put this down tightly a
second okay we're going to take it off
quickly okay got a few little hairs on
the back of that one and this one is the
slow one so it is steady over a 1
1,000 to
1,000 and then we would ask Fran to fill
out a questionnaire in which he would
say whether he had less or more pain or
actually more precisely after I took it
off quickly he would fill out what the
pain level was associated with that and
after I did the other one slowly he
would give me what the pain level was
associated with that so try this on your
own get friend to put two Band-Aids on a
good friend and let that good friend
take it off in the two ways and see how
you would react differently thank you
Julie you're
welcome all right so before we were
ready to say and let's say that Fran
said I'm just I was just reading his
face at that moment so I would think he
like the fast one less pain than the
slow one let's say that's what the data
said that he reported before we would be
ready to say that fast Band-Aid removal
was had a lower pain level than slow
Band-Aid removal I think that there
would be a number of questions that we
would want answered or we would want to
look at either in the paper or talking
to the investigator so what I've done
here is note 12 of those questions that
I think would be like a framework of how
we would go through this paper and each
one of them will sort of deal with a
different aspect of the paper so we can
judge it we can see what the invest
tigators did we can also look at whether
we feel that that was adequate or not or
whether in fact that led to some problem
that we need to take into account in the
interpretation of the findings so the
first question would really be why why
did the investigators do this study the
first thing that we'd want to understand
is let's say they did this study in 2011
in 2011 what was the evidence about the
relationship about fast removal of
Band-Aids and slow removal of bandaids
ades with respect to pain is this the
first study that's ever looked at it is
it the first randomized trial that's
ever looked at it have people looked at
it in different ways but not this
particular question in terms of whether
there's less pain or not so when they
started the study what was the current
state of knowledge so the current state
of knowledge of the evidence about
Band-Aid removal and pain and probably
even more important than that when I try
to look at a study what was the Gap in
knowledge so probably there was some
evidence about this previously Maybe not
maybe this is the first study but if
there was some evidence what had they
shown and what was the next piece of
information that needed to be shown so
the Gap in knowledge that this study is
supposed to fill that would be my first
question to get an idea of the context
the totality of evidence on this
question
and then I would look at the
participants in the study we said that
there were
65 healthy
volunteers why did they pick the number
65 why didn't they pick 200 why didn't
they say they could do this with 10
healthy volunteers that makes a
tremendous difference in terms of the
logistics the cost the Personnel of
doing the study why did they pick the
number that they did how did they decide
how many people to enroll in the study
on what basis did that make the decision
and what is the impact of that in terms
of the interpretation of the
findings third question now let's go to
the exposure remember we said it was
fast removal versus slow removal and I
just sort of said roughly you take it
off very quickly or you take a couple
seconds to do it but was what was the
working definition of the interventions
fast Band-Aid removal FB and slow
Band-Aid removal
SB and how did they make sure that this
SB and FB would done exactly the same in
all 65 of the participants times two
organs each time two arms two ankles two
leg two hands so obviously the person
didn't do it that has the Band-Aids on
them did the investigator do it for
everybody or did he have a staff that
did it if the staff did it how did they
do do it in a uniform way and are we
comfortable that it was done in a
uniform way and it wasn't just that one
person's slow removal was very different
than another person's slow removal how
were they assigned to the intervention
of fast removal versus slow removal what
exactly was
randomized and how did they learn which
one was supposed to be first or which
arm or whether was fast or was going to
be slow how did they get that
information who gave them that
information how was it done what was the
main outcome of the study we said pain
but how is pain measured and was it
measured in a reliable way was it
measured in a consistent way for all the
participants so however they did it did
they do it in a way that instilled
confidence in it in us that this was a
reproducible consistent way to look at
the outcome in this study what was the
main result of the trial
and now let's talk about how to
interpret that
result what about the result just being
due to
chance um maybe we pretty much saw that
you know some of the people said that
fast was less painful than slow but
maybe if there was really no difference
between fast and slow at all you're not
necessarily going to get an even number
of people that report each one is being
better so how do you know that the
results you actually saw were not simply
due to the luck of the draw or sampling
variability how did they measure that in
the study and are you convinced that
it's a a
statistically significant finding not
just something due to the luck of the
draw and then if we showed overall
whatever the result was as we said
before it looked like pain was reduced
in the fast removal but is that true for
everybody or were there some
characteristics of the participants
that made it be that fast versus slow is
better depends on the characteristics of
the participants so is there effect
modification was the effect of fast
versus slow on pain level dependent on
any characteristics of the participants
which would mean that when we make a
recommendation later we would recommend
something differently fast or slow
differently depending on the person who
is asking us the question
now we go beyond the study just a little
bit and we talk about how to generalize
the findings let's say now you've read
that article you mention to a friend
that you've read an article about
Band-Aids and you tell them among these
65 volunteers what you what the results
were and your friend says to you but
wait a minute um I'm a parent and I
really have wanted to know the answer to
this question CU what happens when my
child has a Band-Aid on is there
anything in this article that would be
relevant to me
in terms of whether for my child I
should remove the Band-Aid quickly or
slowly so what would you tell your
friend who is the parent of a small
child and more broadly to whom can the
results of this study be generalized and
on the other side to whom can the result
not be generalized so we have a very
clear idea to whom these results are
applicable and when we have to stop in
terms of our recommendation based on
what we saw in this this
article and then let's go back a little
bit to the beginning of the design of
the study think a little bit about the
ethical considerations of doing this
study or any trial what was it that the
investigators had to think about at the
very beginning in terms of regulations
in terms of guidelines related to ethics
and these would be guidelines that would
protect the investigators but would also
and most importantly protect the
participants in the study what what did
you see in the paper that were aspects
of the study that they built in or
regulations that they had to go through
that were direct consequences of
considering the ethical considerations
of this
study and then finally two last
questions we know a little bit now about
how to design a trial Fran went through
the basic principles and we're going to
be talking about some of them as we go
through these questions is there
anything you would have done differently
If This Were your question and you
wanted answer the question about whether
it was better to do fast versus slow
removal of Band-Aids is there anything
about the study that you would have
built in that you would have changed
that you felt might have lowered Li the
limitations of the study or made it just
a fuller richer study what would you
have done differently and finally the
very last question do you think the
question about speed of Band-Aid removal
and pain is now answered did this trial
answer that question and when we say
answer we mean is there I don't care
whether slow or or fast is better I just
want a definitive answer to the question
either clearly one is better so I now
can make a recommendation to others
about this or in fact definitively there
is no difference between the two which
is a finding in itself because it
basically says don't worry about it
don't try to do it one way or another
just get it done and this is what is
supported by the evidence from this
trial so do you think the question is
now answered or do you think another
investigation would be necessary to
answer it definitively and if so what
would that investigation be so I think
that these are the questions that we
really need to answer over this next
period of time while you're reading the
article and I very much look forward to
seeing the thoughts and the um the
arguments that you put forward on the
discussion forum and my username is
Julie B J L I E B and I'm going to be
very pleased to be reading what you are
writing and at some point coming back to
talk about what I think are the answers
to these questions but in light of the
thoughts that you have put forward on
the discussion forum thank you very
much
